text: Who's on the supply side of your marketplace?
subtext: e.g., 'Independent dog walkers who want extra weekend income.'
why: Clearly defining your supply side shapes the research into how you'll recruit and retain them.

subtext: e.g., 'Dog owners in Chicago who work long hours and need a mid-day walk.'
why: Clearly defining your demand side shapes the research into how you'll reach and convert them.

text: How do you make money on each transaction?
why: How you make money on each transaction is the core of your revenue model and what benchmarks we compare you against.

option_notes.Take rate (commission on each transactio: We'll benchmark typical commission rates for marketplaces like yours.
option_notes.Subscription (from one or both sides): We'll look at subscription pricing and what it takes to get users to commit upfront.
option_notes.Listing fee: We'll look at typical listing fee levels and how they affect supply-side willingness to join.
option_notes.Featured / promoted listings: We'll treat this as an add-on revenue layer and look at typical promoted-listing pricing.
option_notes.Payment processing fee: We'll look at typical payment processing margins and the volume you'd need for it to add up.

text: If commission-based, what percentage per transaction?
subtext: Number between 0 and 100. Skip if you're not using commissions.
why: Your commission percentage drives the revenue model in your report. Example figures here are just examples — your report prices everything in your own currency, using costs for your country.

text: Is there a free side or free tier? What's included?
subtext: Marketplaces often make one side free to solve the chicken-and-egg problem.
why: Knowing which side is free helps us judge how you'll solve the chicken-and-egg problem most marketplaces face.
Where the marketplace operates changes how big a market we size, and what compliance and payments complexity you'll face.

option_notes.One city: We'll keep the market sizing tight and focus on local liquidity between supply and demand.
option_notes.One country: We'll size the market nationally and factor in a single set of compliance and payments rules.
option_notes.English-speaking countries: We'll widen the market sizing and flag the extra complexity of multiple countries' rules.
option_notes.Global: We'll size the market broadly and flag the compliance, currency and payments complexity of going worldwide.

why: Who's building the platform changes your build costs, timeline and technical risk.

option_notes.I code it myself: We'll assume low cash build costs but factor in your own time as the main constraint.
option_notes.I have a technical co-founder: We'll assume low cash build costs, with two people's time as the main constraint.
option_notes.No-code tools: We'll factor in platform subscription costs and the limits no-code tools put on customisation.
option_notes.I'll hire / outsource development: We'll factor in developer costs and the time it takes to brief and manage outsourced work.
